findings.message: Collapsing every parsed command to `cmd.argv.join(" ")` and then filtering out empty strings makes the new `ShellParserPort` path lose redirect-only commands and quoted-argument boundaries. In the usecase adapter, that means inputs like `> /tmp/file` can disappear before policy evaluation, so the guard can no longer block output-redirection writes reliably.

The new `ShellParserPort` implementation at this file is not exercised by any test. The updated tests were switched to `ShellParser::split_shell(...)`, so they still only cover the domain-trait path and never verify the new `Vec<String>` serialization (`argv.join(" ")`) or its error mapping. That leaves the only new behavior in this diff unguarded, so a regression in the infrastructure port would slip through.

P0: serializing each parsed command with `cmd.argv.join(" ")` here is not lossless. Any argument that contains whitespace gets split back into separate argv entries by the usecase adapter, so shell-reentry inputs like `bash -c 'git push origin main'` are reconstructed as `bash -c git push origin main`. That breaks the `-c` payload boundary and can let blocked commands slip past policy checks.

Serializing redirect-only commands as `""` creates a guard bypass in the new CLI path: `ShellParserPortAdapter` will re-parse that entry as `argv: []` with `has_output_redirect = false`, so `domain::guard::policy::check_simple_command` never hits its output-redirect block. A command like `> /tmp/file` will be treated as allowed instead of blocked if this port is used for enforcement.

Both `map_err` calls add a new textual prefix before returning the `String`, but `ExportSchemaInteractor` already wraps any port error as `ExportSchemaError::ExportFailed`. Once this adapter is wired in, failures will surface as `schema export failed: schema export failed: ...` or `schema export failed: schema serialization failed: ...`, which changes the existing user-facing error contract. Return the underlying error text unchanged here and let the usecase layer own the prefix.

`ShellParserPortAdapter` reconstructs `SimpleCommand` with `redirect_texts: Vec::new()` and `has_output_redirect: false`, so the downstream guard policy can no longer see or block output redirects or heredoc-based git references. Once commands cross this new boundary, inputs like `> /tmp/file` are treated as allowed instead of blocked.

P1: `ShellParserPortAdapter` discards the parser's error string and rewrites every failure to `ParseError::UnmatchedQuote`, so `GuardCheckInteractor::check` will always surface the unmatched-quote block reason even when the underlying parser failed for a different cause. That misreports parser failures and defeats the domain's distinction between nesting-depth and syntax failures.

P0: [libs/usecase/src/guard.rs:131](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/usecase/src/guard.rs#L131) reconstructs argv with `split_whitespace()` after the port has already flattened each command into a single string. That destroys shell quoting/escaping, so a command like `git -C "repo dir" commit` is re-tokenized as `git`, `-C`, `repo`, `dir`, `commit`; `extract_git_subcommand()` then skips only one `-C` argument and can misidentify the effective subcommand, allowing guarded git operations to bypass the policy. This is a security-relevant false negative in the new guard boundary.

`block-test-file-deletion` is wired through `ShellParserPortToDomainAdapter`, which reconstructs argv with `split_whitespace()` from the port's lossy `argv.join(" ")` strings. For a guarded command such as `bash -c 'rm tests/foo.rs'`, the parsed shell payload becomes tokens like `bash`, `-c`, `rm`, `tests/foo.rs`; the existing handler then takes only `rm` as the `-c` inner command and sees no test-file argument, so it allows the deletion. This regresses the security-critical test deletion guard once CLI dispatch moves through this service.

`block-direct-git-ops` is routed through the lossy `ShellParserPortToDomainAdapter`, which reconstructs `SimpleCommand` with `redirect_texts: Vec::new()` and `has_output_redirect: false`. When T010 wires CLI dispatch through this service, commands that the existing hook blocks via the full domain parser, such as `echo x > src/lib.rs` or other Bash output redirects, will be seen as plain argv and allowed, bypassing the file-write guard policy.

`HookDispatchInteractor::new` exposes `domain::guard::ShellParser` in the public usecase API instead of the T002 usecase-owned `ShellParserPort` described in the briefing. That means the CLI wiring for this service will still have to depend on the domain parser trait rather than staying behind the usecase boundary; add an internal adapter (or usecase-owned faithful parser port) and keep the constructor typed to the usecase port.

`lib.rs` now declares `pub mod hook_dispatch;`, but `libs/usecase/src/hook_dispatch.rs` is untracked and absent from the git diff. If this change is committed as-is, consumers will get a module-not-found compile failure for `hook_dispatch`. Add the new module file to version control with the `lib.rs` change.

`NextTaskOutput` and `TaskCountsOutput` are missing fields that the current track CLI output already relies on: next-task prints the task `status`, and task-counts prints `total`. As written, this service cannot faithfully replace the existing behavior without recomputing those values outside the usecase boundary.

`schema_version` is hard-coded to `3`, so `resolve_phase` will misclassify older v1/v2 tracks where branchless planning should still resolve to `Planning` instead of `Ready to Activate`. This wrapper needs the real schema version from metadata rather than assuming the current one.

Every port failure is mapped to `ExportFailed`, so the public `SerializationFailed` variant is never reachable even though the error type and docs promise it. The infrastructure adapter already distinguishes export vs serialization in its error strings, so the interactor should preserve that distinction.

P1: `TaskOperationInteractor` never enforces the branch guard that these commands advertise. `skip_branch_check` is ignored in all four mutation methods, and the dedicated `BranchGuardFailed` / `BranchlessGuardFailed` variants are never emitted, so task mutations can still run on non-track branches instead of being rejected. See [task_ops.rs](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/usecase/src/task_ops.rs#L118) and [task_ops.rs](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/usecase/src/task_ops.rs#L240).

P1: `TrackPhaseInteractor::resolve` hardcodes `schema_version = 3` and ignores `items_dir`, but `resolve_phase` uses the real schema version to decide between `Planning` and `ReadyToActivate`. Any track whose stored schema version is not 3 will be reported with the wrong phase and next command. See [track_phase.rs](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/usecase/src/track_phase.rs#L145).

`TrackPhaseInteractor::resolve` always passes `schema_version = 3` into `resolve_phase`. That collapses the schema-version branch in `domain::track_phase`, so a branchless v2 track is reported as `Ready to Activate` instead of `Planning` (the domain layer still exercises the v2 case).

P1: `read_schema_version_from_metadata` silently converts any missing or malformed `metadata.json` into schema version `5`, so a corrupt track can be reported as a valid v5 track instead of failing. That can change the resolved phase output and hide filesystem/data corruption. This should propagate an error through `TrackPhaseError` instead of defaulting.

P1: Branch validation now shells out to `git` directly from usecase code. That makes `TaskOperationService` depend on the host Git installation and checkout state, and it cannot be mocked in unit tests. This violates the repo's boundary/testability rules; the branch source should be injected behind a port or resolved in the composition root.

P1: `TrackPhaseInteractor` now reaches straight into `metadata.json` with `std::fs::read_to_string`/`serde_json` instead of going through the existing store/port boundary. That adds a hidden filesystem dependency to the usecase layer, makes the service impossible to exercise with only mocked `TrackReader`/`ImplPlanReader`, and breaks the hexagonal rule that infrastructure access stays behind traits.

P1: `verdict_to_output` is `pub` and its signature exposes `domain::review_v2::ReviewApprovalVerdict`. That leaks a domain type into the public usecase API, which defeats the boundary the rest of this bundle is trying to establish and lets downstream code depend on domain review types through the usecase crate.

`transition_task`, `add_task`, `set_override`, and `clear_override` all persist the mutation and then call `load_impl_plan()` only to compute `derived_status`. If that second read fails, the method returns `StoreFailed(...)` after the write has already succeeded, so callers observe a failure for a committed change and may retry into a duplicate or inconsistent state. Make the status lookup non-fatal, or perform it before the write if you need it to gate the operation.

`TaskOperationInteractor` swallows `load_impl_plan` errors after a successful write (`ok().flatten()`), so `transition_task`/`add_task`/`set_override`/`clear_override` can return `Ok` with a derived status computed as if the plan were missing. A transient repository read failure is hidden and the CLI can report the wrong post-mutation status. Propagate the error or derive status from the mutation result instead.

`TrackPhaseInteractor::new` hardcodes `schema_version = 5` when no reader is injected. That makes phase resolution wrong for existing schema v2/v3/v4 tracks; for example, a branchless v2 planned track will be reported as `Ready to Activate` instead of `Planning`. The interactor should require the real schema version or fail closed when it cannot read it.

After each successful mutation, `transition_task`/`add_task`/`set_override`/`clear_override` does a second `load_impl_plan()` and turns any read failure into `StoreFailed`. That lets the method return `Err` after the write has already committed, so a transient read error can prompt a duplicate retry against an already-mutated track.

`BranchReaderFn` is exported as a new public boundary type, but the approved usecase catalogue does not include it. That leaks an undocumented dependency past the usecase boundary and means branch-guard enforcement depends on hidden wiring instead of the published `TaskOperationService` contract.

`SchemaVersionReaderFn` is exported as a new public boundary type, but the approved catalogue only models `TrackPhaseOutput`, `TrackPhaseError`, `TrackPhaseService`, and `TrackPhaseInteractor`. A CLI wired from the documented contract cannot supply this extra reader without undocumented glue, so the public API no longer matches the catalogue.

P1: `TaskOperationInteractor::new` always stores `branch_reader: None`, and the only constructor that can inject a reader is `#[cfg(test)]`. In real builds that makes `skip_branch_check = false` a no-op, so branch mismatches are never rejected and mutations can run on the wrong checkout.

P1: `TrackPhaseInteractor` has the same production-only gap: `with_schema_version_reader` is test-only, so the only runtime path falls back to `schema_version = 5`. That will misclassify older tracks, which the code itself notes can change the phase/next-command result.

`TrackPhaseInteractor` falls back to a hard-coded `schema_version = 5` here, so any legacy branchless track that is still on an older schema will be classified with the wrong phase (`Ready to Activate` instead of `Planning`). The public API therefore returns incorrect results unless every caller remembers to inject a reader via `with_schema_version_reader`.

The post-write `load_impl_plan` result is discarded with `.ok().flatten()` here, which converts repository/read failures into a successful response with a fabricated "no impl plan" status. That means callers can get a committed mutation back with a stale or wrong `derived_status` and no signal that the projection refresh failed; the same pattern repeats in the other mutation methods.

Public constructors expose less-visible function type aliases in their primary API. `ReviewCheckApprovedInteractor::new` takes the private `CheckApprovedBuildFn` alias here, and the same pattern appears for `VerifySpecRefsRunFn`, `TypeSignalsRunFn`, `BranchReaderFn`, and `SchemaVersionReaderFn` in the new scoped files. Because the `usecase` crate opts into workspace lints with `warnings = "deny"`, rustc's `private_interfaces` warning will be promoted to a compile error when the crate is checked. Make these aliases public API types or inline the `Arc<dyn Fn...>` signatures / use public wrapper traits so the constructors no longer mention private aliases.

`enforce_branch_guard` explicitly returns `Ok(())` for branchless tracks, and `transition_task` then mutates via `execute_by_status` without the existing `reject_branchless_guard`/schema-version activation check. A CLI migrated to this service can transition schema v3+ planning-only tracks to implementation statuses before activation; activation later rejects branchless tracks with in-progress plans, leaving the workflow in an invalid state. Enforce the branchless activation guard before the mutation, with a real schema version source in the usecase boundary.

This new public wiring alias exposes `domain::TrackId` in the usecase API even though the boundary contract for `ReviewCheckApprovedService` takes a `String` specifically so CLI code does not import domain IDs. Since the CLI composition root must construct this interactor, the constructor signature leaks the domain type across CN-01 and is not part of the approved usecase catalogue. Keep the domain conversion inside usecase or make the injected closure use only standard/usecase-owned types.

`SchemaVersionReaderFn` is a public usecase API that takes `&TrackId`, adding an undocumented public boundary type and leaking `domain::TrackId` through `TrackPhaseInteractor::with_schema_version_reader`. This contradicts the catalogued `TrackPhaseService` boundary, whose inputs are `String`/`PathBuf`, and forces external wiring to depend on domain types to get correct schema-version-aware phase resolution.

`NextTaskOutput` only carries `task_id` and `description`, but the current `track next-task` CLI response also includes the selected task's `status`. Once the CLI is migrated to this service, it cannot preserve the existing JSON contract without either dropping `status` or re-reading domain task state outside the usecase boundary. Include the task status in this DTO and populate it from `t.status().kind()` in `next_task`.

`NextTaskOutput` exposes a public `status` field even though the approved usecase catalogue declares only `task_id` and `description` for this DTO. The briefing requires `expected_members` to match strictly, so this changes the published boundary contract for `TaskQueryService::next_task`. Remove the extra member or update the catalogue before wiring CLI consumers against it.

Missing task transitions are never converted to `TaskOperationError::TaskNotFound`. `TransitionTaskUseCase::execute_by_status` returns `TrackWriteError::Domain(DomainError::Transition(TransitionError::TaskNotFound { .. }))`, but this conversion collapses every domain error into `TransitionFailed`, leaving the declared `TaskNotFound` boundary variant unused for the actual missing-task case. Match that domain error explicitly and return `TaskNotFound`.

`set_override` persists the override and then reads `impl-plan.json` only to compute the display status. The existing CLI path deliberately derives this status from the override alone, so an unreadable/corrupt impl plan now returns `StoreFailed` after metadata was already mutated, making the caller see a failed operation even though the override was applied. Keep the old behavior here, or read before mutating if the plan is truly required.

`add_task` now rejects an invalid optional `after_task_id` by returning `InvalidTaskId`. The current CLI behavior explicitly treats an invalid `--after` as `None` and appends the task, so wiring this usecase service would turn previously successful add-task commands into failures. Preserve the fallback before delegating to `AddTaskUseCase`.

`add_task` silently discards an invalid `after_task_id` by converting `TaskId::try_new(a)` with `.ok()` and then proceeds to append the new task. That violates the no-silent-error-swallowing rule and can mutate `impl-plan.json` in the wrong order when a caller mistypes `--after`; this should return `TaskOperationError::InvalidTaskId` instead of treating the input as absent.

`TrackPhaseInteractor::new` installs a schema-version reader that always returns `5`, so this valid `TrackPhaseService` instance misclassifies legacy branchless tracks whose stored schema version is not 3..=5. For example, a schema v2 planned track should resolve to `Planning`, but the default interactor reports `Ready to Activate`; require an injected real schema-version source or fail closed instead of guessing.

The public task-operation constructors make guard enforcement optional: `TaskOperationInteractor::new` sets both guard readers to `None`, so a normal service instance ignores `skip_branch_check = false` and allows branch mismatches and branchless planning-track implementation transitions. Since the catalogued mutation commands expose guard errors, the interactor should require the guard ports needed to enforce them rather than silently disabling them in the default constructor.

P1: `transition_task` calls `reject_branchless_guard` before the explicit not-found path, then maps every failure from that guard to `BranchGuardFailed`. If the track does not exist, callers get the wrong error variant instead of `TrackNotFound`, which breaks downstream error handling and makes a missing track indistinguishable from a real branch-policy failure.

P1: the only constructor that can inject a branch reader is `#[cfg(test)] pub(crate) fn with_branch_reader`, while `TaskOperationInteractor::new` always stores `branch_reader: None`. In non-test builds that makes `skip_branch_check = false` a no-op, so branch mismatches are never rejected by this service.

`TaskOperationInteractor::new()` now hard-codes `branch_reader: None`, and the only constructor that can inject a branch reader is `#[cfg(test)]`. In non-test builds `enforce_branch_guard()` therefore takes the no-op path, so `skip_branch_check = false` no longer rejects branch mismatches or detached HEAD state and task mutations can run on the wrong checkout.

Forwarding `git`'s raw stderr into the user-facing error leaks checkout/path details on every branch-reader failure. This should be normalized to a generic message or sanitized before crossing the usecase boundary.

`TaskOperationInteractor::new()` now hardcodes a `git` subprocess and `with_branch_reader()` is only compiled for tests, so non-test callers can no longer inject a branch source. That breaks the trait-boundary design and makes branch-guarded mutations fail at runtime outside a real git checkout or without `git` installed.

P1: [task_ops.rs](./libs/usecase/src/task_ops.rs:230) / [task_ops.rs](./libs/usecase/src/task_ops.rs:295) now hardwires `read_current_branch()` into the default constructor, and that helper shells out to `git` via `std::process::Command` inside the usecase layer. This reintroduces an infrastructure dependency behind the usecase boundary instead of keeping branch detection behind the injected port, and the happy-path test was changed to `skip_branch_check = true`, so the new production path is no longer exercised in tests.

`TaskOperationInteractor::new()` still leaves `branch_reader` unset, so `enforce_branch_guard()` returns early and every mutation on that instance silently skips the branch check. Because this constructor remains public, any production caller that uses it can mutate a track on the wrong checkout with `skip_branch_check = false` and no error.

`ReviewCheckApprovedInteractor::check_approved` has no behavior test covering its public path. The current tests only exercise `verdict_to_output` and error-variant construction, so the new track-id validation and closure delegation here can regress without a failing test.

The branchless-track coverage here no longer exercises the production constructor: this test uses `with_branch_reader`, which is `#[cfg(test)]` and sets `schema_version_reader: None`, so it bypasses the new `TaskOperationInteractor::new(..., schema_version, ...)` wiring that actually enforces the planning-only guard. A regression in `new` could therefore silently stop rejecting schema v3..=5 branchless transitions without failing any test. Add a test that uses `new` on a branchless track and asserts the guard outcome.

The new production path in `TaskOperationInteractor::new` is only exercised for the branchless-activation case. All branch-mismatch and detached-HEAD assertions still go through the test-only `with_branch_reader`, so a regression in `new` that drops or miswires the injected branch reader would not be caught by the suite. Add a `new`-based branch-guard failure test.

`TrackPhaseInteractor::new` is only tested with schema version 5. The legacy v1/v2 path described in the constructor docs is still only exercised via the test-only `with_schema_version_reader`, so a regression that ignores the caller-provided schema version or hardcodes 5 would slip through. Add a `new`-based test for a non-5 legacy track.

Both [spec_refs.rs](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/usecase/src/catalogue_verify/spec_refs.rs#L169) and [type_signals.rs](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/usecase/src/catalogue_verify/type_signals.rs#L150) only cover the happy path for their new public wrappers. There is no test that an `Err` returned by the injected closure is propagated unchanged, which leaves the public error contract unverified. Add one failure-path test for each wrapper.

Step 12a only checks that `plan.md`, `contract-map.md`, and `<layer>-types.md` exist, so an old `cargo make track-sync-views` result can still satisfy the gate. That means skipping step 11 can leave stale rendered views on disk while the final post-condition still claims the outputs are “freshly regenerated”.

The recovery path on `verify catalogue-spec-signals` failure is an unconditional `step 9 -> step 11 -> step 12b` retry with no attempt cap or escalation. If the coverage mismatch persists, the agent can loop indefinitely, which is the failure mode the briefing explicitly asked to avoid.

Step 11 now explicitly regenerates `plan.md`, but the new 12a glob check never verifies it. That leaves one of the `cargo make track-sync-views` outputs able to stay missing or stale without tripping the final gate, so the stated "all outputs are freshly regenerated" post-condition is not actually enforced.

The freshness gate only re-validates `catalogue-spec-signals`; it never checks the `type-signals` artifact from step 10. By inference, a skipped or stale `type-signals` run can still leave `<layer>-types.md` incomplete while 12a/12b both pass, so the new self-verify does not cover all pipeline outputs.

P1: The new mandatory 12b gate (`bin/sotp verify catalogue-spec-signals`) is not reflected in the Bash allowlist. The Scope Ownership/Bash usage section still lists only the `track ...` subcommands plus `track-sync-views`, so an agent following the whitelist literally can treat the freshness check as out of scope and skip it. See [/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/.claude/agents/type-designer.md#L526](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/.claude/agents/type-designer.md#L526) and [/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/.claude/agents/type-designer.md#L532](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/.claude/agents/type-designer.md#L532).

P1: Step 12a is internally inconsistent about how many paths it verifies. The block enumerates 9 paths total (7 per-layer paths plus `contract-map.md` and `plan.md`), but the closing sentence says “all 8 expected paths.” That mismatch makes the final self-check contract ambiguous and easy to misapply. See [/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/.claude/agents/type-designer.md#L181](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/.claude/agents/type-designer.md#L181).

Step 12 still only proves freshness for `catalogue-spec-signals.json`; the graph outputs, `plan.md`, `contract-map.md`, and `<layer>-type-signals.json` are only checked for existence. A stale artifact from an earlier run can still satisfy 12a/12b, so the new post-condition that all prior outputs are “freshly regenerated” is not actually enforced.

P1: Step 12a still does not prove `<layer>-types.json` was freshly produced in this session. The only source-of-truth artefact in the existence check is globbed, so a stale catalogue file left over from a prior run will satisfy the guard even if step 7 was skipped. That leaves the exact skipped-step/stale-artifact failure mode this change is meant to block. Consider requiring a same-session write receipt for step 7, or a content/freshness check on `<layer>-types.json`, not just existence.

Step 11 now requires `cargo make track-sync-views`, and the step explicitly says it renders `plan.md`, but Scope Ownership still lists `plan.md` under writes forbidden and Rules still say not to modify it. An agent following the lower section can either skip the new required sync step or treat its required `plan.md` update as a policy violation, leaving rendered views stale.

The Step 9 command is documented as `bin/sotp track catalogue-spec-signals <id>` without the supported `--layer <layer_id>` filter, even though this pipeline is per layer in scope. For a single-layer run, this command processes every catalogue-spec-enabled layer and can fail on unrelated missing/stale catalogues outside the assigned layer, blocking the scoped Phase 2 pass.

Step 9 is documented as `bin/sotp track catalogue-spec-signals <id>` with no `[--layer <layer_id>]`, unlike the surrounding per-layer pipeline commands. The CLI supports `--layer`; without documenting it, a type-design run scoped to a single layer will regenerate all catalogue-spec signal files and can fail on or modify out-of-scope layers. This breaks the layer scope contract and can block otherwise valid single-layer Phase 2 runs.

Step 11 now requires `cargo make track-sync-views`, and the new self-check requires `track/items/<id>/plan.md` to exist, but the Scope Ownership/Rules section still forbids modifying `plan.md`. Since `track-sync-views` renders `plan.md`, the agent instructions are contradictory: a compliant agent can treat the required Step 11 write as forbidden and skip or fail the refreshed-view step the policy is trying to enforce. Clarify that `plan.md` must not be edited directly, but may be regenerated by the required `cargo make track-sync-views` command.

Step 12b is meant to verify freshness for the explicit track `<id>`, but the documented command has no track argument and relies on the current git branch to choose what to verify. The existing `verify catalogue-spec-signals` command skips successfully on non-`track/*` branches and would verify a different track if the checkout is on a different track branch, so this policy can let the agent emit a final message without checking the `<id>` it just processed. Require a precondition that the current branch resolves to the same `<id>` and treat a SKIP/mismatch as failure, or use a track-bound verification command.

The Positive consequence says the CLI will depend on usecase only, but D1 explicitly keeps `infrastructure` in `apps/cli`'s `may_depend_on`, and Rejected Alt C rejects banning cli→infrastructure. This is a semantic contradiction in the ADR's stated dependency boundary; the consequence should be narrowed to domain-facing data/operations going through usecase, or otherwise account for the retained infrastructure dependency.

The Neutral consequence says existing grandfathered `cli→domain` references are unaffected, but D1 removes `domain` from `apps/cli` dependencies, D2 migrates all 25 direct references in one track, and D3 explicitly avoids any transitional allow list. That leaves the ADR with conflicting design intent about whether any existing CLI-to-domain references may remain grandfathered.

`task-coverage.json` does not trace the three GO-* requirements from [spec.json](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/track/items/cli-via-usecase-only-2026-04-30/spec.json:5) at all: this file only maps `in_scope` / `out_of_scope` / `constraints` / `acceptance_criteria`, so the briefing claim that every spec element is linked to at least one task is not actually satisfied.

`task-coverage.json` stops its `out_of_scope` map at `OS-05`, but `spec.json` defines `OS-06` (`cli` 以外の delivery adapter 追加). That leaves one declared out-of-scope requirement with no task coverage, so the coverage artifact is incomplete.
The new D1 record says it captures OQ-1〜OQ-6, but the subsection only documents OQ-1, OQ-2, OQ-3, OQ-4, and OQ-6. OQ-5 is never recorded, so the claimed permanent boundary record is incomplete.

Section S4 says the review boundary batch includes `ReviewRoundType`, but T005 does not list it. Since `ReviewRoundType` is also catalogued in `usecase-types.json`, the plan is internally inconsistent about what that task is meant to create.

S1 makes T001 remove the cli→domain layer dependency first and explicitly says the build will break until later tasks restore it. That contradicts the ADR decision that the `architecture-rules.json` and `apps/cli/Cargo.toml` changes happen last after the usecase boundary and CLI migration are in place, and undermines the no-transitional-allowance rationale in D3. The implementation plan should either keep the enforcement flip last or update the ADR/spec to record the changed sequencing intent.

The track task catalogue defines mutation command DTOs such as `TaskTransitionCommand`, `AddTaskCommand`, `SetOverrideCommand`, and `ClearOverrideCommand`, but the only task-operation application service catalogued here is `TaskQueryService`, whose methods cover only `next_task` and `task_counts`. As written, T006 does not provide a usecase public API that can consume those mutation commands, so T011 cannot migrate the track mutation commands away from domain-typed usecase calls without inventing uncatalogued services or continuing to expose domain types.

T003 omits `HookDispatchError` from the hook dispatch boundary types it is supposed to add, but `usecase-types.json` defines `HookDispatchError` and `HookDispatchService.dispatch` returns `Result<HookVerdictOutput, HookDispatchError>`. As written, the task can be completed as described while leaving the catalogued service signature without its required error type, so the plan and catalogue disagree on the required implementation surface.

T012 is planned to migrate review classify/files by wiring the existing `ScopeQueryService`, but the referenced service API still exposes `ScopeName` in `files(scope: ScopeName)` and returns/accepts `FilePath`-based values. The same entry says this migration is meant to remove direct `domain::review_v2::...ScopeName` imports, while IN-03/CN-01 require usecase-owned DTO/command/query/result types at the public boundary. As written, the plan leaves the review classify/files boundary dependent on the very domain review types this track is supposed to hide; add a strict usecase-owned string/DTO wrapper or include the needed boundary types before T012.

`RunReviewCommand` is described as carrying `briefing_file`, but `expected_members` only declares `track_id`, `round_type`, `group`, `model`, `timeout_seconds`, `base_prompt`, and `items_dir`. That makes the command catalogue internally inconsistent and leaves the run-review CLI input surface under-specified for T005/T012; either declare the `briefing_file` member or remove it from the required command payload if it is not actually part of the boundary.

T012 says the classify/files migration will add string-accepting ScopeQueryService adapter methods or a dedicated facade so CLI handlers pass Vec<String>/String and never import domain::review_v2::FilePath, ScopeName, or MainScopeName, but the usecase catalogue still records ScopeQueryService as the boundary with methods taking Vec<FilePath> and ScopeName and returning FilePath-based results. That leaves the planned public usecase API exposing exactly the domain review types the plan says must be hidden, so the plan artifacts contradict D1/CN-01 and do not describe an executable CLI migration for these commands.

`ScopeQueryService` is catalogued as a public `application_service`, but its expected methods still expose `Vec<FilePath>`, `ScopeName`, `Vec<FilePath>`, and `PathClassification` while the same entry says those domain review types are hidden behind the usecase boundary. This contradicts CN-01/IN-03's requirement that usecase public APIs expose only usecase-owned DTO/Command/Query/Result types, and leaves T012 able to add string wrappers without actually removing the domain-typed public boundary.

ADR D1 records the review-run boundary as `RunReviewCommand` carrying only string/primitive values, but the catalogue defines `RunReviewCommand.round_type` as the usecase enum `ReviewRoundType`. That makes the CLI consume a boundary enum for this command instead of the recorded string/primitive interface, so the plan contradicts the ADR decision it is implementing.

The plan says the CLI composition root will wire `ConchShellParser` into `GuardCheckInteractor`, while this adapter is only recorded as implementing `domain::guard::ShellParser` and is a `reference`/no-change infrastructure type. The corresponding usecase entry says `GuardCheckInteractor` is constructed with an `Arc<dyn ShellParser>`. With `apps/cli` losing its domain dependency and AC-03 banning CLI `domain::guard::ShellParser` imports, T010 has no domain-free way to perform this wiring unless the plan also adds a usecase-owned port or an infrastructure/usecase factory that hides the domain trait. As written, the DI plan contradicts the strict boundary and is not executable.

`ReviewRoundType` is described as something the CLI consumes directly, but `RunReviewCommand` later says `round_type` is a `String` specifically so the CLI never imports `ReviewRoundType`; this leaves the run-review boundary contract contradictory.

`ShellParserPort` says the interactor receives a usecase-owned port and then "casts to the domain trait" when calling domain guard logic. A `dyn ShellParserPort` cannot be cast into an unrelated `domain::guard::ShellParser` trait object; the plan needs an explicit adapter/delegation strategy or a different port shape, otherwise this boundary is not implementable as described.

The schema-export boundary does not define a usecase-owned port even though this track removes CLI access to `domain::schema::SchemaExporter`. The catalogue says `ExportSchemaInteractor` holds a `SchemaExporter` secondary port and delegates to `domain::schema::SchemaExporter`, while the spec requires usecase public APIs to expose only usecase-owned DTO/Command/Query/Result types and IN-04 explicitly lists `domain::schema::SchemaExporter` as a CLI import to eliminate. As written, the plan either exposes the domain port through the interactor construction boundary or leaves the CLI-to-infrastructure wiring undefined. Add a usecase-owned schema exporter port/adapter pattern, analogous to `ShellParserPort`, or otherwise clarify how the adapter is injected without leaking the domain trait.

`ConchShellParser` is described as being wired as `Arc<dyn usecase::ShellParserPort>`, but the catalogue marks the adapter as `action: "reference"` and its `implements` list only contains the old `ShellParser` trait. Because `ShellParserPort` is newly introduced by this plan, the adapter needs an explicit modify entry/implementation; otherwise the S1/T010 wiring is not executable and the generated contract map also omits the `ConchShellParser -> ShellParserPort` implementation edge.

T012 says the new string-based `ScopeQueryService` methods are implemented in `ScopeQueryInteractor`, and the service description says domain value construction happens inside that interactor, but `usecase-types.json` has no `ScopeQueryInteractor` modify/reference entry. The catalogue therefore does not represent a type that the plan explicitly requires changing, leaving the contract map stale/incomplete for the review-scope boundary design.

S9/S10 require the CLI migration to “wire new interactors”, but the usecase catalogue only defines interactors for GuardCheck, ExportSchema, HookDispatch, TrackPhase, and ScopeQuery. The services needed by these sections, such as TaskOperationService, TaskQueryService, PreCommitTypeSignalsService, CommitHashPersistenceService, RunReviewService, and the verify services, have no corresponding implementation/interactor entry or task ownership. An implementer following this plan cannot complete the CLI migration without inventing unplanned usecase types.

The infrastructure catalogue marks RustdocSchemaExporter as modified to implement the new SchemaExporterPort, and ConchShellParser is similarly modified for ShellParserPort, but the implementation plan scopes T002/T004 to usecase-layer additions and later CLI tasks only wire the adapters. No task owns the required infrastructure impl changes, so the planned Arc<dyn SchemaExporterPort>/ShellParserPort wiring cannot compile from the listed tasks alone.

`GuardCheckInteractor` is described as holding an injected `Arc<dyn ShellParserPort>`, but its catalogue contract declares `expected_members: []`. The same pattern appears on other interactors that are supposed to hold injected ports/services. Since `expected_members` is the contract for interactor struct fields, the plan currently specifies zero-field interactors while also requiring dependency injection, making the boundary implementation internally inconsistent and potentially unwireable from the CLI composition root.

`VerifyAdrSignals` is being kept as a referenced usecase boundary for T012, but its public method still returns `AdrVerifyReport` instead of a usecase-owned DTO/Result type. That contradicts CN-01's requirement that CLI-consumed usecase APIs expose only usecase-owned DTO/Command/Query/Result types, and it leaves the review/verify migration plan unable to satisfy the stated “replace all domain imports with usecase boundary types” goal without adding a wrapper/output type for ADR signal verification.

T011's actionable task description omits `commands/track/tddd/`, but the same S9 section explicitly includes `commands/track/.../tddd/` and the spec calls out `domain::tddd::*` as part of the imports to remove. This contradiction can cause the implementation task to skip the tddd track commands, leaving AC-03/IN-04 incomplete.

`AdrVerifyOutput` says the CLI uses `red_count >= 1` for the non-zero exit code “per AC-01”, but AC-01 is the architecture-rules dependency criterion, not an exit-code/verify behavior criterion. This is a broken cross-reference in the catalogue and points implementers at the wrong acceptance criterion; it should either cite the external-behavior criterion (AC-10) or drop the AC reference.

The `VerifyAdrSignals` entry references `VerifyAdrSignalsCommand` and `VerifyAdrSignalsError`, and the later `ScopeQueryService` entry references `ScopeQueryError`, but none of those types are defined anywhere in this catalogue. That leaves the usecase contract internally inconsistent and makes the T012 migration path incomplete until the missing boundary types are added or the signatures are rewritten to existing types. The generated markdown mirror repeats the same gap.

`TrackPhaseInteractor` and `TaskOperationInteractor` are both described as depending on storage "ports" (`TrackReader + ImplPlanReader` for phase resolution, and the `FsTrackStore` port for task operations), but this plan never introduces those ports. The only storage-related infrastructure node is `FsTrackStore` as a `secondary_adapter`, and the contract map only adds `ShellParserPort` and `SchemaExporterPort`. As written, S4/S5 cannot be implemented as a proper hexagonal boundary without adding unplanned types, so the downstream CLI migration tasks are underspecified.

`TaskOperationInteractor` now says the CLI composition root injects `FsTrackStore` directly "without importing it as a port", but `TaskOperationService` still describes the interactor as persisting via the `FsTrackStore` port, and `contract-map.md` models `FsTrackStore` as a `secondary_adapter`. The same type is being described with two different architectural roles, so the plan artifact is internally inconsistent.

`task-coverage.json` omits coverage for the three `goal` spec elements (`GO-01`, `GO-02`, `GO-03`). The spec declares 28 elements and the rendered spec shows these goals without any `[tasks: ...]` mapping, so the plan does not satisfy the stated requirement that every spec element is linked to at least one task.

`VerifyAdrSignals`/`VerifyAdrSignalsInteractor`/`AdrVerifyOutput` are marked as `modify` in the usecase catalogue, but `impl-plan.json:170-176` and `plan.md:70-72` never allocate a usecase-side task for that API change. T012 only updates the CLI callers and explicitly says to use the existing `VerifyAdrSignals` service, so the catalogue and implementation plan disagree about whether this round changes the verify-ADR-signals boundary or only its consumers.

`T012` and the mirrored `S10` text say `ScopeQueryService::classify_by_strings` returns `Vec<ScopeClassificationOutput>` and `files_by_string` returns `Vec<String>`, but the authoritative `ScopeQueryService` contract in `usecase-types.json` returns `Result<..., ScopeQueryError>` for both methods. As written, the plan drops the error channel and makes the API shape inconsistent across the artifacts.

`VerifyAdrSignals` and `ScopeQueryService` both reference boundary types that are never defined in this catalogue: `VerifyAdrSignalsCommand`, `VerifyAdrSignalsError`, and `ScopeQueryError`. Because the plan relies on those names in the modified method signatures, the usecase contract is not self-contained and the T012 migration surface is incomplete until those types are added or the signatures are rewritten to existing catalogued types.

The usecase catalogue requires concrete interactors such as ReviewCheckApprovedInteractor and RunReviewInteractor, but the corresponding review implementation task only lists the service/output/error/command types and never schedules those interactors. The same omission repeats for several other catalogue interactors in the task-operation, verification, pre-commit, and commit-hash groups, while later CLI migration sections say to wire the new interactors. As written, the plan leaves required catalogue types unscheduled and makes the implementation path incomplete unless the implementer invents extra work outside the task plan.

The infrastructure catalogue is inconsistent with this review scope's stated design intent that domain/infrastructure entries are references only. `RustdocSchemaExporter` is marked `action: "modify"` here, and `ConchShellParser` is likewise marked `modify` later, because the plan adds usecase port impls to infrastructure adapters. That leaves the plan unclear on whether infrastructure changes are in scope for this track.

The command boundary is internally contradictory: the surrounding descriptions say CLI-to-usecase commands carry only string/primitive fields, but these command objects include `PathBuf` fields such as `items_dir` here, and the same pattern appears for `RunReviewCommand` (`briefing_file` / `items_dir`). Implementers cannot tell whether paths should cross the usecase boundary as strings or as `PathBuf`.

The plan still exposes domain traits through usecase interactor APIs: TaskOperationInteractor is specified as generic over `domain::TrackReader + domain::TrackWriter + domain::ImplPlanReader + domain::ImplPlanWriter` (and TrackPhaseInteractor does the same at line 886). That contradicts CN-01/D1's requirement that domain types not appear in the usecase public API; removing only `pub use domain::...` in T009 would not address public generic bounds. The plan needs a usecase-owned port/factory boundary here, or another design that keeps those domain traits out of the public usecase surface consumed by the CLI.

`ConchShellParser` is marked and described as an in-scope infrastructure modification that adds the `ShellParserPort` implementation required by IN-04, but its `spec_refs` point only to `CN-04` and `OS-05`. `OS-05` is the out-of-scope guarantee that domain internals are unchanged, so this entry has no in-scope trace for the actual modification, unlike the parallel `RustdocSchemaExporter` entry. Either reference IN-04 here or stop treating this adapter as modified.

`RustdocSchemaExporter` and `ConchShellParser` are declared as infrastructure `action: modify` entries and justified as “required by IN-04”, but IN-04 only scopes replacing CLI-side domain imports, while IN-03 only scopes new usecase boundary types. The impl plan also schedules these infrastructure port impl additions in T002/T004, so the artifacts disagree about whether infrastructure adapter changes are actually in scope for this track. Add an explicit in-scope/AC item for the infrastructure `*Port` impls, or remove those modifications from the plan/catalogue.

`plan.md` still says S1 groups guard and hook because they "share the ShellParser secondary port", but the new `HookShellParserPort` entry makes the hook path depend on a distinct port. That leaves the section description and T003 scope out of sync with the catalogue, so the plan no longer describes the architecture it is meant to implement.

`usecase-types.json` says `ConchShellParser` implements `HookShellParserPort`, but the rendered contract map only shows `ConchShellParser -> ShellParserPort` and has no edge for the new port. The catalogue and contract map therefore disagree about the adapter ownership of `HookShellParserPort`.

HookShellParserPort is described as being used by hook dispatch, but the same entry then says "CallerPort callers (GuardCheckInteractor) receive the full struct." GuardCheckInteractor belongs to the guard path, so this misstates the consumer and mixes the two responsibilities.

ConchShellParser's prose says this track adds only the ShellParserPort impl, but the machine-readable `implements` list also adds HookShellParserPort. The narrative underreports the actual adapter change and is out of sync with the contract.

HookShellParserPort is the only new catalogue entry that still carries `informal_grounds`, which contradicts the plan-artifacts rule that this round's catalogue should not retain informal grounds after the ADR handoff.

`HookShellParserPort` is introduced as a usecase public port, but its description and signature explicitly expose `domain::guard::SimpleCommand` and `domain::guard::ParseError`. That conflicts with `CN-01` / `IN-03`, which require the usecase public API to avoid direct domain-type exposure, so this port reintroduces the boundary leak the track is trying to remove.

T003 is still scoped as a usecase-only hook-dispatch task, but the updated plan summary and the infrastructure catalogue now assign `HookShellParserPort` plus the `ConchShellParser` wiring to the same work. A literal implementation of T003 would leave that new port/adapter change unowned, so the task is not self-contained.

D1 still states that the usecase public API should not expose domain types and should use DTO/Command/Query/Result types instead, but this new subsection makes `HookShellParserPort` a public usecase port whose signature directly exposes `domain::guard::SimpleCommand` and `ParseError`. The exception is named, but the ADR never reconciles it with the original D1 rule, so the decision text now conflicts with itself.

HookShellParserPort is added as a usecase public secondary port, but its signature returns domain guard types (`Result<Vec<SimpleCommand>, ParseError>`) and the description explicitly says those are `domain::guard::SimpleCommand` and `domain::guard::ParseError`. This contradicts the same entry's CN-01/IN-03 coverage and ADR D1's Direction X boundary, which require usecase public APIs consumed from CLI to expose usecase-owned DTO/Command/Query/Result types rather than domain types. As written, T003 blesses a domain-type leak through the usecase API instead of satisfying the strict CLI-via-usecase boundary.

`HookShellParserPort` is added as a public usecase secondary port whose signature returns `Result<Vec<SimpleCommand>, ParseError>` and the description explicitly says those are `domain::guard` types. That contradicts the same plan's CN-01/D1 direction that usecase public API must not expose domain types, and T009 only removes `pub use domain::` re-exports, so this signature exposure would remain. Implementers are left with conflicting architecture requirements for whether this port is allowed.

`ConchShellParser` is now described as adding both `ShellParserPort` and `HookShellParserPort` implementations “scoped by IN-06”, but IN-06 only scopes the existing `ShellParserPort`/`SchemaExporterPort` adapter implementations and its task coverage is T002/T004. The new T003 hook parser adapter work is therefore being justified by a spec item that does not actually cover it, leaving the plan/spec trace for this infrastructure change inconsistent.

`ConchShellParser` says both the `ShellParserPort` and `HookShellParserPort` impls are “scoped by IN-06”, but `spec.json` defines IN-06 only for `ShellParserPort`/`SchemaExporterPort` and adds IN-07 specifically for the `HookShellParserPort` impl. This leaves the catalogue entry with conflicting scope attribution for the hook parser work; the hook impl should be attributed to IN-07, not IN-06.

`HookShellParserPort` is documented as the CN-01 exception for the hook-dispatch path, but its `spec_refs` only point to `IN-03` and `IN-04`. That leaves the new exception port without a direct anchor to the constraint/rationale that justifies exposing `SimpleCommand` and `ParseError`, so the catalogue trace for this special-case type is incomplete.
